original_abstract: Arabic is characterized by a unique and intricate linguistic style across its written, spoken, and arbitrary forms. Within the vast domain of Arabic studies, morphology—essentially known as the science of *sharaf*—serves as a fundamental pillar for understanding word structures. This study employs a library research method to characterize the linguistic and morphological features of Surah An-Naba’ in the Qur'an. Specifically, the research aims to identify the occurrences of *fi'il mudhari* (present tense verbs) within the 30th Juz of the Qur'an, analyzing their specific reading patterns and the *dhomir* (pronouns) associated with each verb. By applying both *nahwu* (syntax) and *sharaf* (morphology) analytical approaches, the study seeks to provide a comprehensive grammatical description of these forms. The findings reveal 16 distinct instances of *fi'il mudhari* in Surah An-Naba’, categorized through various *wazans* (morphological patterns) and conjugated with different pronouns. These results offer valuable insights for students and scholars navigating the complexities of Quranic Arabic.
humanized_abstract: Arabic language, broadly defined, has a distinctive and intricate linguistic system for its written, spoken and arbitary forms, therefore, the science of morphology which is called Sharaf in Arabic is one of the corner stones in linguistics of Arabic language and consequently in Arabic grammar. This study makes use of a library research as aims at identifying the linguistic and morphological features that are presented in Surah An-Naba’ that belongs to the 30th Juz of the Holy Qur’an, and identifying all the occurrences of *fi’il mudhari* in the present tense in Surah An-Naba’, the readings, the *dhomir* (pronouns) that are attached to each of these verbs and presenting these forms by applying the rules of *nahwu* and *sharaf* and as such by giving a full grammatical explanation of these verbs. The study discovered that there are 16 *fi’il mudhari* in Surah An-Naba’ which have been identified according to the* wazans* (morphological patterns) to which they belong, and the* dhomir* (pronouns) that are attached to each of them. These discoveries are consequently of great utility for the students and researchers in the field of linguistics of the Holy Qur’an, Arabic language, as well as for those who study the grammar of the Arabic language.
